coredata.dc:title: On-off-on fluorescent nanosensing: Materials, detection strategies and recent food applications
coredata.dc:creator: He, Haoyang
Sun, Da-Wen
Wu, Zhihui
Pu, Hongbin
Wei, Qingyi
coredata.dc:description:
Background The detection of food quality and safety is important in the whole food supply chain. In recent years, fluorescent detection technology has been extensively applied in food analysis. In particular, nanomaterial-based “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors have received much attention because of their high sensitivity, wide detection range, strong anti-interference ability, and flexible design. Nevertheless, there is no review focusing on the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors for food analysis.
Scope and approach In the current review, several common nanomaterials are introduced, including quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and metal nanoclusters (MNCs). Main detection strategies of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are elaborated, involving redox reaction, competitive reaction, DNA hybridization reaction and enzymatic reaction. In addition, recent applications of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors for trace level detection of nutrients, food additives, drug residues, pesticide residues, foodborne pathogenic bacterias, mycotoxins, and heavy metal ions are also summarized. Finally, advantages, challenges and future prospects for developing the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are presented.
Key findings and conclusions The “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors exhibit a wide detection range, strong anti-interference ability, and excellent linear responses to various analytes. However, current detection applications are still at the laboratory stage, commercial and industrial applications are rarely reported. Future research should focus on developing high-throughput detection techniques, and portable or visual fluorescent sensors, including kits and filter papers based sensors. Apps on smartphones should also be developed in future work.

Fluorescence recovery

(A) Schematic illustration of the N, S-CDs/Fe3+ system for the detection of AA (Luo et al., 2018); (B) Schematic illustration of the detection process for TBHQ (Yue et al., 2016); (C) The first sensing type of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors based on DNA hybridization reaction; (D) Schematic illustration for the sensor-based FRET between multicolour fluorescent QDs nanoprobes and MWCNTs@GONRs for dual detection of cDNAs in transgenic soybean (Li, Sun, et al., 2017); (E) The second sensing type of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors based on DNA hybridization reaction; (F) Schematic illustration of the principle for PPase activity detection based on CuNCs reversible nano-switches (Ye et al., 2019).

(A) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence assaying for thiamine detection (Purbia & Paria, 2016); (B) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence assaying based on the employment of DNA-AgNCs and Cu2+ for the detection of l-histidine (Zheng et al., 2015); (C) Schematic illustration of fluorescence assaying for thiram detection (Chu et al., 2020); (D) Schematic diagram of the smartphone sensing platform (Chu et al., 2020); (E) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence assaying for PA detection by using the strategy of redox reaction (Qu, Na, et al., 2018); (F) Schematic illustration of detecting principle of melamine based on β-CD-CNPs (Liao et al., 2021).

(A) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence assaying for OTC detection based on an indirectly labeled OTC aptamer (Yuan et al., 2015); (B) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence assaying for the determination of PM (Luo et al., 2017); (C) Schematic illustration of the “on-off-on” AuNCs-based fluorescent sensor for the rapid detection of E. coli (Yan et al., 2018); (D) Schematic illustration of sensing platform for OTA detection based on ZnCdSe QDs (Liu et al., 2020); (E) Schematic illustration of the GO-based sensor system for Hg2+ detection (Cui et al., 2015); (F) Schematic illustration of sensing platform for Pb2+ detection based on GQDs and GOs (Qian et al., 2015).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of food quality and safety is important in the whole food supply chain. In recent years, fluorescent detection technology has been extensively applied in food analysis. In particular, nanomaterial-based “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors have received much attention because of their high sensitivity, wide detection range, strong anti-interference ability, and flexible design. Nevertheless, there is no review focusing on the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors for food analysis.

In the current review, several common nanomaterials are introduced, including quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and metal nanoclusters (MNCs). Main detection strategies of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are elaborated, involving redox reaction, competitive reaction, DNA hybridization reaction and enzymatic reaction. In addition, recent applications of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors for trace level detection of nutrients, food additives, drug residues, pesticide residues, foodborne pathogenic bacterias, mycotoxins, and heavy metal ions are also summarized. Finally, advantages, challenges and future prospects for developing the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are presented.

The “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors exhibit a wide detection range, strong anti-interference ability, and excellent linear responses to various analytes. However, current detection applications are still at the laboratory stage, commercial and industrial applications are rarely reported. Future research should focus on developing high-throughput detection techniques, and portable or visual fluorescent sensors, including kits and filter papers based sensors. Apps on smartphones should also be developed in future work.

ce:section.ce:para: Food quality and safety is a global concerning issue, which plays a vital role in the processing and storage of foods and has a considerable influence on human health (Han, Hao, et al., 2020). Food quality and safety analyses involve the monitoring and assessment of various food compositions, such as amino acids, peptides, vitamins, and phytochemicals, and the monitoring of harmful substances, such as banned food additives, drug and pesticide residues, mycotoxins, foodborne pathogenic bacteria, and heavy metal ions (Peltomaa et al., 2021; Hussain et al., 2020a). With increasing health awareness, rapid and reliable detection of food quality and safety has become an indispensable part of the whole food supply chain, which not only provides warnings to protect consumers from the threats of spoilage, contamination and adulteration but also contributes to constructing an efficient food management system (Luo et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020).

Conventional food quality and safety assaying methods, such as Kjeldahl nitrogen apparatus, thin layer chromatography (TLC), gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer (LC-MS), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), have been widely employed to detect proteins, vitamins, amino acids, and various harmful substances in foods owing to their high reproducibility, fabulous susceptibility and selectivity (Tao et al., 2020; Hussain et al., 2020b; Hu et al., 2021). However, these methods suffer from the drawbacks of complicated sample pretreatment, expensive instruments, labour-intensive, and well-trained personnel, which greatly restrict their applications. Additionally, the complicated food matrix will interfere with the accuracy of detection (Lan et al., 2017; Lv et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2022). Consequently, it is necessary to develop real-time and rapid methods to ensure that consumers receive up to date information on food quality and safety (Medina et al., 2019; Jayan et al., 2021).

In recent years, spectroscopic techniques such as hyperspectral imaging technique (Cheng & Sun, 2017; Ma & Sun, 2020; Pu et al., 2019) and Raman chemical imaging technique (Wang et al., 2019a; Wu et al., 2020; Hussain et al., 2021; Pu et al., 2021; Wang, Sun, et al., 2021) have been widely studied as novel detection techniques with remarkably shortened detection time and improved sensitivity. On the other hand, nanomaterial fluorescent assaying techniques (Wei et al., 2020, 2021) has also emerged as an innovative detection method and has attracted widespread attention in the field of food detection and analysis. With the techniques, food components and contaminants can be effectively determined at the nano level. Compared with traditional methods, the nanomaterial fluorescent assaying technique has inimitable advantages such as ultralow detection limit, real-time rapid assaying, larger detection range, and low cost (Lv et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2021).

Fluorescent nanomaterials, with sizes around 1–100 nm, include metal-based quantum dots (QDs), metal nanoclusters (MNCs), carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), etc., which have been effectively verified as excellent fluorescent probes for analytes owing to their high photoluminescence (PL) intensity, photobleaching resistance, controllable synthesis, easy surface modification, and cost-effectiveness (Chandan et al., 2018). With the continuous studies on the structures and properties of these nanomaterials, their PL can be improved based on optimising the properties of cores and surface functional groups in different synthetic methods (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018). In addition, QDs, CDs, and MNCs can be also modified with aptamers for the selective determination of more analytes (Zhou et al., 2020).

Conventional fluorescent sensing is mainly based on “on-off” fluorescent sensors. In such sensors, the original fluorescence of the nanomaterials is quenched directly by mixing the nanomaterials and analytes. The analytes can then be successfully determined by monitoring the extent of fluorescence quenching. Such “on-off” sensors are popular in detecting metal ions and natural organics due to their simple operation, short detection time, and low cost. However, the anti-interference ability of “on-off” sensors is comparatively poor because some matrices especially those similar to analytes can also quench the fluorescence, resulting in a decrease in detection accuracy at trace levels, while some analytes cannot be quantified directly due to their weak binding affinity with fluorescent nanomaterials. Therefore, to bridge the shortcomings of “on-off” sensors, numerous researchers utilized “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors to indirectly detect analytes (Luo et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016). For “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors (Fig. 1 A), the fluorescence of nanomaterials can be quenched via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or inner filtering effect (IFE). Upon addition of analytes, the fluorescence of nanomaterials can be recovered via redox reaction, competitive reaction, DNA hybridization reaction or enzymatic reaction. As such, the intensity of fluorescence restoration can be utilized to determine the concentration of analytes. Generally, the specific reaction of analytes and quenchers disrupt the initial quenching interaction between fluorescent nanomaterials and quenchers, leading to the release of nanomaterials and restoration of fluorescence. Thus the anti-interference ability and detection accuracy can be greatly improved (Liu et al., 2020). Such an indirect detection approach has been extensively developed to detect nutritional components and harmful substances in foodstuffs. However, up to now, there is no review concentrating on the application of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors in food quality and safety analysis.

Therefore, in the current review, common fluorescent nanomaterials and their optical properties are introduced and the main detection strategies of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are described, involving redox reaction, competitive reaction, DNA hybridization reaction and enzymatic reaction. Furthermore, the applications of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors in food quality and safety detection are reviewed, including the established methods, linear response ranges, limits of detection (LODs), and feasibility in real food sample applications. In addition, current challenges and prospects of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are also highlighted. It is hoped that this review could provide instructive enlightenment for the further establishment and practical applications of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors.

The common fluorescent nanomaterials in “on-off-on” nanosensing include quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and metal nanoclusters (MNCs). Table 1 summarises the comparison of the synthesis methods, advantages, and limitations of the fluorescent nanomaterials (see Table 1).

Quantum dots refer to nano-crystals of semiconducting materials owning quantum mechanical performances with sizes from 2 to 10 nm and they can radiate different colours according to their sizes (Tandale et al., 2021). Different from conventional organic dyes, QDs possess distinctive properties including narrow emission bands, brighter fluorescence, resistance to photo-bleaching, and excellent optical properties, which are conducive to the simultaneous imaging of multiple fluorophores (Jaiswal & Simon, 2004). The PL wavelength of QDs can be adjusted by changing their sizes owing to the quantum confinement effects and the broad absorbance. Narrow emission and larger Stokes shift of QDs are prone to the assaying of multiple targeted analytes (Labiadh & Hidouri, 2017). The synthesis methods of QDs can be divided into top-down and bottom-up methods. The top-down methods include e-beam lithography, molecular beam epitaxy (MBE), X-ray lithography, and ion implantation, while the bottom-up method refers to the colloidal QDs that are synthesized by self-assembly in solution after chemical reduction (Valizadeh et al., 2012). However, the toxicity of some semiconductor QDs is a non-negligible factor in analytical sensing. Some QDs such as CdSe, CdTe, PbS, etc. are prone to cause secondary pollution to foodstuffs attributed to the contained heavy metal ions. With the improvement of preparation methods, QDs have overcome some of their inherent shortcomings and their adaptability has been improved for versatile bioconjugation, further enlarging the application in analytical sensing (Chandan et al., 2018).

CDs have attracted wide attention in food analysis in the past decades because of the non-blinking fluorescence emission and ready solubility with the major advantage of low cytotoxicity, ease of synthesis, and convenient surface functionalization (Lim et al., 2015). The structure of CDs is sp2 conjugated carbon cores that are inserted in amorphous shells (mainly sp3 hybridized carbon matrices), which generally contain numerous oxygen/nitrogen-based groups or polymeric aggregations (Luo et al., 2020). As a novel class of multifunctional nanomaterials, CDs have been in the spotlight of enormous applications in food science (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018). The synthesis methods of CDs can be divided into top-down approaches such as arc discharge, laser ablation, and chemical ablation and bottom-up methods such as hydrothermal treatment and microwave irradiation (Wang, Lu, et al., 2017). As the unique PL feature of CDs is the emission wavelength and intensity dependence on the excitation wavelength, various kinds of CDs exist with different PL properties due to the discrepancies of synthesis methods, sizes, and excitation wavelengths (Sun et al., 2006). However, synthesizing CDs excited by red or near-infrared sources is a major challenge and the photoluminescence quantum yield of CDs is comparatively lower than semiconductor QDs. Generally, the fluorescence quantum yields of CDs is relatively low and the heteroatomic doping such as N, S, and B of CDs can improve radiative recombination and obtain higher quantum yield (Pan et al., 2020).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a newcomer to the nanocarbon materials family, which are derived from graphene or graphene oxide. GQDs are small graphene sheets with lateral dimensions less than 10 nm and with less than 10 graphene layers forming the nanoparticle (Yan et al., 2019) and they exhibit attractive physical and chemical properties including stable PL, chemical inertia, perfect solubility, better surface grafting, and low cytotoxicity, which ensure their potential practicability for food quality and safety detection. Meanwhile, the single atomic layer plane conjugate structure of GQDs provides a large specific surface area and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface provide more active sites to absorb more active molecules. The synthesis approaches of GQDs include oxidative cleavage, hydrothermal or solvothermal method, ultrasonic-assisted or microwave-assisted process, and electrochemical oxidation (Chen et al., 2018). As the PL of GQDs is caused by the combination of competition effects of intrinsic state emission and defect state emission, similar to CDs, the discrepancies in sizes, excitation wavelengths, pH, as well as different synthetic methods lead to their distinct PL properties (Li et al., 2013). However, the photoluminescence quantum yield is lower and the near-infrared photoluminescence behaviour is relatively rare compared with QDs (Ghaffarkhah et al., 2021).

Metal nanoclusters (MNCs) are composed of several to hundreds of metal atoms with sizes less than 2 nm and they are the transition state from atomic regime to plasmonic metal nanoparticles. Different from metal nanoparticles, one of the characteristics of MNCs is that the free electrons from metal atoms cannot oscillate collectively when an electric field is present. Thus, they have strong PL and distinct step-like absorption bands due to quantized energy levels, which offer excellent photostability, biocompatibility, and solubility (Maity et al., 2019). Among special properties of MNCs, the most superior feature is fluorescent behaviours due to the great potentials for designing probes, imaging dyes, photosensitizers and light-emitting devices (Li, Chen, & Mei, 2017). MNCs are usually prepared by chemical reduction of metal ions precursors in the presence of template molecules. Some common templates such as DNA, peptides and proteins, polymers can be utilized to improve the stability and PL intensity. Besides, the etching method is another approach to prepare MNCs, with which large metal particles are etched into ultra-small MNCs by adding etching agents such as mercaptan and glutathione (Zhao et al., 2019). Meanwhile, the PL properties of MNCs can be influenced by metal cores, the valence state of metal atoms, metal-ligand shells, and the nature of thiolate ligands. Besides, the aggregation of MNCs results in stronger coprophilic interaction of intra- and inter-MNCs and restrains the intramolecular rotation and vibration of capping ligands, thus enhancing the PL intensity and quantum yield. However, MNCs are usually unstable without a proper stabilizing template. Also, the size of MNCs is uncontrollable and the fluorescence is not tunable (Kang & Zhu, 2019).

The “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are developed to indirectly detect analytes by introducing the quenchers. The fluorescence of nanomaterials is initially quenched via FRET, PET or IFE, the addition of analytes can restore the fluorescence through the strategies of redox reaction, competitive reaction, DNA hybridization reaction or enzymatic reaction. In the typical FRET process (Fig. 1B), the nonradiative transfer of excitation energy from the nanomaterials (mainly CDs and QDs) to the proximal ground-state acceptor (quenchers) leads to fluorescence quenching of nanomaterials. PET process (Fig. 1C) refers to the transferring of photon excited electrons from nanomaterials to analytes, which results in less excited electrons returning from excited states to the ground state and thus the fluorescence is quenched. The PET process mostly occurs between CDs and metal ions. For the IFE process (Fig. 1D), the absorptive spectra of analytes overlap with the emission spectra and the emitted light of nanomaterials is rapidly absorbed by the analytes, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Ji et al., 2020).

For this strategy, quenchers are generally highly oxidizing substances and analytes are usually the substances with strong reducibility. When analytes are introduced to the quencher-nanomaterial systems, they can be oxidized by the quenchers. Furthermore, the quenchers lose their binding affinity with nanomaterials, which weakens the intensity of FRET, PET or IFE. As a result, the fluorescence of nanomaterials will be restored. The intensity of fluorescence restoration can then be used for the sensing of analytes.

The redox reaction strategy has been employed in several studies to detect vitamins, food additives, phytochemicals, etc. Luo et al. (2018) made the most of N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) to establish an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor to quantify ascorbic acid (AA) in pear and apple samples (Fig. 2 A). In their study, Fe3+ ions were introduced into N, S-CDs solution, the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ was then occupied by the electrons in the excited state of N, S-CDs, causing the remarkable fluorescence quenching (PET effect). The addition of AA led to the reduction of Fe3+ into Fe2+ and thus the fluorescence of N, S-CDs was recovered because Fe2+ ions had no obvious quenching effect on N, S-CDs. Likewise, AA was also determined by using the CDs as a fluorescent assaying platform because the fluorescence of CDs could be dynamically quenched by Cr6+ due to the IFE process and recovered with the introduction of AA owing to the redox reaction between Cr6+ and AA (Zheng et al., 2013). In addition, based on the redox reaction between dichromate ions (Cr2O7 2−) and dopamine, Wu et al. (2018) designed an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for the rapid determination of dopamine. The fluorescence of P, N co-doped carbon dots (P, N-CDs) was effectively quenched by Cr2O7 2− via the IFE process. Subsequently, dopamine could trigger the decomposition of Cr2O7 2−, which could be reduced to Cr3+ and the fluorescence of P, N-CDs was recovered.

In the redox reaction strategy, the redox reaction between quenchers and analytes leads to the recovery of fluorescence. Nevertheless, competitive reaction strategy generally refers to the specific coordination reaction between quenchers and analytes that leads to the recovery of fluorescence. In this strategy, nanomaterials can be modified with active functional groups or ligands on their surface, such as –NH2, –COOH, –OH, aptamer, etc., which normally react with quenchers and accompany the fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, analytes can restore the fluorescence by competitive combination with the quenchers due to their high binding affinity with the quenchers. Consequently, the concentration of analytes can be facilely determined by monitoring the intensity of fluorescence restoration.

Based on this strategy, Yue et al. (2016) designed an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for the determination of tertiary butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) in edible oils (Fig. 2B). Firstly, Fe3+ ions (the quenchers) coordinated with –COOH and –OH groups on the surface of CDs, the excited electrons of the CDs entered the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ and the fluorescence of CDs was dynamically quenched (PET effect). Upon the addition of TBHQ, the active TBHQ molecules then competed with the CDs for Fe3+, which led to the release of Fe3+ due to the strong coordination reaction between Fe3+ and TBHQ and accompanied the fluorescence restoration, and the intensity of fluorescence recovery was in proportion to the concentration of TBHQ. Moreover, Yu et al. (2018) reported an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor based on the strong affinities between Cu2+ and d-penicillamine. Firstly, 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid was modified on the surface of Au nanoclusters (MUA-AuNCs) via an Au–S bond to improve water solubility and photostability, the fluorescence of MUA-AuNCs was then remarkably quenched by Cu2+ through PET effect. Upon the addition of d-penicillamine, the Cu2+ was captured by d-penicillamine, which led to the recovery of fluorescence. This method exhibited better behaviour for d-penicillamine determination.

DNA hybridization reactions are generally applied in the “on-off-on” sensors, which can be divided into two types. For the first type (Fig. 2C), the binding affinity of two complementary single-stranded DNAs is much stronger than that of a single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) with the quencher, attributed to the unique base complementary pairing principle. Thus, when the target DNA strand is present, double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) can be formed and separated from the quencher. As such, the fluorescence of already-quenched QDs is easily restored due to the strong complexation tendency of the complementary DNA strands. On the basis of this, Li, Sun, et al. (2017) proposed an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for monitoring dual-target DNAs of promoter cauliflower mosaic virus 35s (P35s) and terminator nopaline synthase (TNOs) to identify the transgenic soybean (Fig. 2D). Initially, the capture DNAs were absorbed on QDs to enhance PL, the π–π stacking between ssDNA probes and multiwalled carbon nanotubes@graphene oxide nanoribbons (MWCNTs@GONRs) resulted in the FRET effect and thus the fluorescence of QDs was quenched. Upon the addition of analytes (P35s and TNOs), the exposed analytes were recognized by dual fluorescent probes to form dsDNA through the hybridization reaction of target DNAs and ssDNA probes, which released the dsDNA and led to fluorescence recovery. Consequently, the detection of P35s and TNOs was realized by the degree of fluorescence recovery.

For the other type (Fig. 2E), the cDNA (complimentary with partial aptamer) is firmly immobilized on QDs, which can further increase their fluorescent intensity. Then, the hybridization of aptamer DNA and cDNA results in the fluorescence quenching of the QDs attributed to the FRET effect. Upon the addition of analytes, aptamer DNA can specifically recognize the analytes, leading to the recovery of fluorescence. As such, the analytes can be facilely determined. Jiao et al. (2018) developed a typical “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for acetamiprid detection. Firstly, the acetamiprid aptamer was labeled on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via amide bonds. The hybridization of the aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA) could then induce the fluorescence quenching of CQDs through the FRET effect. In the presence of acetamiprid, the duplex structure of aptamer-cDNA switched to aptamer-acetamiprid complex, which led to the release of the cDNA-CQDs and accompanied the recovery of fluorescence. The detection of acetamiprid in their study was realized and verified in lettuce samples.

Due to the simplicity and specificity of enzymatic reactions, enzymes can be utilized for the fluorescent sensing process. The substrate as quencher initially decreases the fluorescence of nanomaterials, and with the introduction of the desired enzyme, the fluorescence of nanomaterials is effectively recovered on account of the decomposition of substrate. For instance, Liu et al. (2015) established an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor based on enzymatic reaction to detect hyaluronidase, in which hyaluronic acid-coated gold nanoparticles (negatively charged) initially quenched the fluorescence of CDs (positively charged), the introduction of hyaluronidase then caused the decomposition of hyaluronic acid and thus restored fluorescence of CDs. Similarly, Ye et al. (2019) proposed a fluorescent analyzing platform to detect inorganic pyrophosphatase based on enzymatic reaction (Fig. 2F). First, the aggregation of copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) triggered by Al3+ resulted in a dynamical fluorescence enhancement. Upon the introduction of pyrophosphate (PPi), the CuNCs–Al3+ complex was disaggregated and accompanied by a remarkable fluorescence quenching of the system owing to the strong coordination reaction of Al3+ and PPi. Subsequently, in the presence of pyrophosphatase, the CuNCs aggregated again due to the decomposition of PPi, which led to the fluorescence recovery.

In addition, the enzyme activity can be inhibited by some analytes (mainly pesticides). Based on this, enzymes can also be used as quenchers to decrease the fluorescence of nanomaterials. With an increase in analyte concentrations, the enzyme activity can be inhibited dynamically, and the enzymes thus lose their binding affinity with nanomaterials, resulting in the restoration of fluorescence. Therefore, the analytes can be determined based on the recovery of fluorescence intensity. Accordingly, Hou et al. (2015) utilized tyrosinase to quench the fluorescence of CDs to indirectly determine methyl parathion (MP), which ascribed to the inhibition of MP on tyrosinase activity that effectively restored the fluorescence of CDs. Chen et al. (2019) used poly-thymine30 DNA-templated copper nanoparticles (poly T30-CuNPs) to construct a fluorescent sensing platform to determine paraoxon in tap water. Initially, the fluorescence of poly T30-CuNPs was remarkably quenched by tyrosinase. When paraoxon was present, the fluorescence was effectively recovered because paraoxon could suppress the activity of tyrosinase and affected the quenching towards the fluorescence of poly T30-CuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in food quality and safety detection
ce:section.ce:para: The applications of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are widespread in the food quality and safety field, involving in the detection of nutrients, drug residues, pesticide residues, banned additives, foodborne pathogenic bacteria, mycotoxins, and heavy metal ions. Table 2 summarises these applications.

Nutrients refer to the chemical components in foods that can offer energy, repair tissues, constitute bodies and regulate physiological functions, and can maintain the health and development of the human body (Panda et al., 2018). The detection of nutrients is an obligatory factor in the process of food quality monitoring. The “on-off-on” sensors have been widely applied in the determination of nutrients, which performs well in real samples and covers an extensive range including vitamins, amino acids, glutathione (GSH), etc.

As a kind of indispensable nutrient, vitamins play a vital role in regulating metabolism. Insufficient or excessive intake of vitamins will induce serious harm to the human body. Purbia and Paria (2016) developed an “on-off-on” sensor derived from luminescent CDs to determine thiamine, known as vitamin B1 (Fig. 3 A). The Cu2+ ions worked as quenchers to interact with CDs, the non-radiative electrons in excited states of CDs transferred to the d orbital of Cu2+, which deactivated the fluorescence of CDs via the PET effect. Upon the introduction of thiamine, the Cu2+ ions competitively captured thiamine because of their strong binding affinity and the PET effect could be greatly weakened. Thus, the fluorescence of CDs was gradually enhanced as the increasing of thiamine concentrations, which exhibited a dynamically linear range from 10 to 50 μM with a LOD of 280 nM.

Besides, Xu et al. (2019) and Raveendran et al. (2019) took the advantage of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors to determine ascorbic acid (vitamin C) based on the redox reactions between the quenchers and ascorbic acid, which exhibited high sensitivity in orange and lemon samples and acquired satisfying LODs of 0.57 μM and 79 nM, respectively.

By and large, the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors are mostly used to detect ascorbic acid and thiamine, there are few studies focusing on the detection of other vitamins. Thus, attempts should be made to construct “on-off-on” sensors for detecting other vitamins.

Amino acids are the basic units of constituting protein, and their detection methods based on “on-off-on” sensors are diverse. Many amino acids, including tryptophan (Pundi et al., 2021), histidine (Han, Mo, et al., 2020), cysteine (Wang, Zhang, et al., 2021), aspartic acid (Karami et al., 2021) have been quantified by using “on-off-on” sensors. Zheng et al. (2015) constructed a fluorescent assaying platform to determine l-histidine based on DNA-templated silver nanoclusters (DNA-AgNCs) (Fig. 3B). The specific interaction between Cu2+ and DNA led to the fluorescence quenching of DNA-AgNCs. Meanwhile, Cu2+ could coordinate with the imidazole group of l-histidine. In the presence of l-histidine, the Cu2+ was released from DNA and competitively coordinated with l-histidine. Accordingly, the fluorescence of DNA-AgNCs was gradually restored and the quantitative analysis of l-histidine showed a linear range of 0.20–80 μM with a LOD of 4.3 nM.

Meanwhile, it was revealed that “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors could be used to detect GSH owing to the strong affinity between GSH and Hg2+, Iqbal et al. (2018) established a fluorescence assaying platform for the quantification of GSH. Initially, the fluorescence of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was dynamically quenched by Hg2+ via the PET effect. After the introduction of GSH, Hg2+ competitively coordinated with GSH and formed the Hg2+-S or Hg2+-NH bond, which induced the recovery of fluorescence. The sensors indicated a high selectivity towards GSH with a LOD as low as 40 nM. Additionally, Gao et al. (2019) also utilized “on-off-on” sensors for the detection of GSH based on the competitive reactions between quenchers and GSH, which exhibited intense sensitivity with LODs as low as 6.32 × 10−4 μM.

Phytic acid (PA) is a kind of phytochemical extensively existing in cereal grains, which is the primary storage form of phosphorus. As a natural antioxidant product, it plays a vital role in normal physiological processes (Marolt & Kolar, 2021).

Qu, Na, et al. (2018) made the most of glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) for PA detection in corn grain samples based on redox reaction (Fig. 3C). To start with, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was availably quenched by Fe3+ with high selectivity on account of the PET effect. In the presence of PA, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH-Fe3+ complex was dynamically restored because Fe3+ could be reduced to Fe2+ and brought out the release of Fe2+. Consequently, an “on-off-on” sensor was constructed to determine PA with a wide linear response range of 0.05–3 μmol/L and the LOD of 14 nmol/L. Similarly, another “on-off-on” sensor was proposed by Gao et al. (2015) to utilize the fluorescence of CDs for the determination of PA via competitive reaction, which presented high sensitivity and selectivity in the corn grain samples with a LOD of 0.36 μM.

As an active alkaloid component, caffeine is conducive to the secreting gastric acid, diuresis, and stimulating central nervous system. However, the excessive intake of caffeine can lead to the headache, anxiety, and insomnia (Choi et al., 2013).

Nemati et al. (2018) utilized sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) to establish an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for caffeine detection in green tea and coffee samples. In their study, the unfilled d orbital of Cu2+ was occupied by the excited electrons of S-CQDs, thus the fluorescence of S-CQDs was dynamically quenched by Cu2+ through PET process. After the addition of caffeine, Cu2+ competitively coordinated with the oxygen and nitrogen atoms of caffeine molecule, which induced the recovery of fluorescence. Their method exhibited a linear response range of 0.2–70 μM with a LOD of 0.05 μM.

Food additives can be abused in the food industry, leading to possible high risks of various cancers, therefore their detection during food processing, storage and transportation is indispensable (Li et al., 2020). As the result, the application of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors for food additives detection is extensive.

Melamine is a kind of organic chemical raw material that can induce renal failure, and it can be artificially added into dairy products by some illegal merchants to raise the nitrogen-containing content (Wang et al., 2019b). Up to now, several researchers have established methods for the determination of melamine in dairy products based on “on-off-on” sensors and acquired satisfying LODs and recoveries.

Jahan et al. (2013) proposed an “on-off-on” sensor for the detection of Sudan red III (carcinogenic dye) based on the strategy of the redox reaction, in which protoporphyrin as the quencher firstly combined boron/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanodots (B, N-CDs) to effectively inactivate the fluorescence of B, N-CDs. The addition of Sudan red III could recover the fluorescence of B, N-CDs owing to the redox reaction between Sudan red III and protoporphyrin (strong oxidizability). The feasibility was verified in tomato ketchup and red chilli sauce samples with a LOD of 90 fM.

Moreover, Kong et al. (2020) reported a sensing platform based on redox reaction to sensitively detect nitrite (NO2 −) in sausage and water. The IFE between graphitic carbon nitrite quantum dots (g-C3N4 QDs) and Fe2+-bathophenanthroline complex (BPS–Fe2+) triggered the fluorescence quenching of g-C3N4 QDs. Upon the addition of NO2 −, the Fe2+ was oxidized by nitrite to form the BPS-Fe3+ complex, which resulted in the fluorescence recovery of g-C3N4 QDs. The assaying method exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards NO2 − with a linear response ranging from 2.32 to 34.8 μM.

Recently, Liao et al. (2021) constructed a fluorescent sensing platform for the determination of melamine based on competitive reaction (Fig. 3D). First, the fluorescence of β-cyclodextrin modified carbon nanoparticles (β-CD-CNPs) could be effectively quenched by Fe3+ and formed β-CD-CNPs-Fe3+ complex. Upon the addition of melamine, the fluorescence of β-CD-CNPs was recovered owing to the strong binding affinity between β-CD cavity and melamine, which led to the release of Fe3+ from the β-CD cavity and restored the fluorescence. Their method exhibited a LOD of 6.82 ng/mL and obtained a satisfying recovery in milk samples.

The application of drugs is strictly regulated for food safety reasons as the presence of drugs in animal-derived foods (such as meat, milk, eggs, or honey) may result in potential risks to human health (Chicoine et al., 2020). Therefore, the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors have been extensively employed for the determination of drug residues.

Yuan et al. (2015) constructed an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor based on DNA hybridization reactions to detect oxytetracycline (OTC) in milk and tap water (Fig. 4 A). Initially, indirect fluorescein (FAM) labeled probe was developed by the partial hybridization of an OTC long-chain aptamer with a FAM-labeled short-chain ssDNA (S1). Then, the aptamer probe was adsorbed on the graphene sheet through the strong π-π stacking. As such, the fluorescence of FAM on the probe was quenched by the graphene sheets attributed to the FRET effect. However, the fluorescence could be recovered again by adding OTC due to the higher affinity of OTC and OTC aptamer than that of S1 and OTC aptamer, which led to the release of the quenched fluorescein and S1. Subsequently, S1 hybridized with the complementary ssDNA (C1) and escaped from the quencher to the solution, leading to the recovery of fluorescence. The reported sensor exhibited a linear response range of 0.01–0.2 μM with a LOD of 0.01 μM.

Another fluorescent platform was established to determine clenbuterol in pork samples by Liu et al. (2017). In this study, the CDs as the fluorescence donors were quenched by gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on account of the FRET effect. The aromatic aniline group (-NH2) and aliphatic imine group (–NH–) in clenbuterol molecule were positively charged and they could be adsorbed on the surface of citrate-stabilized AuNPs through electrostatic interaction, which led to the aggregation of AuNPs. Therefore, the clenbuterol rescued AuNPs from the coverage of CDs and recovered the fluorescence of CDs. Under optimal conditions, the LOD for clenbuterol detection in pork samples was 3 nM, with a large linear range of 8–200 nM. Additionally, Guo et al. (2019) utilized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as fluorescence sensing platform and Cu2+ ions as quenchers to construct an “on-off-on” sensor to detect enrofloxacin in tap water, which exhibited a satisfying linear range of 1–15 μg/mL with a LOD of 0.16 μg/mL.

The abuse of pesticides induces severe public health concerns because pesticides inhibit the catalytic activity of certain enzymes and result in high toxicity (Hu & Yang, 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Hussain et al., 2020c), and the “on-off-on” sensors can be universally applied to detect pesticides.

Wang, Bi, et al. (2016) developed an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for glyphosate detection in cereal samples. Firstly, the fluorescence of CDs was dynamically quenched by silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) attributed to the IFE process. After the introduction of glyphosate, the negatively charged glyphosate induced the aggregation of AgNPs (positively charged) through electrostatic interaction. The absorption of AgNPs then decreased, thus the fluorescence of CDs was restored. The method showed a linear response in the range of 0.025–2.5 μg/mL with a LOD of 12 ng/mL.

Luo et al. (2017) utilized bovine serum protein-protected gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) as a sensing platform for the detection of parathion-methyl (PM) in apple and cucumber samples (Fig. 4B). First, thiocholine was produced owing to the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine iodide by acetylcholinesterase (AChE). Then the Au–S bonds were formed by the strong covalent effect between the sulfhydryl groups (-SH) on thiocholine and BSA-AuNCs, which could cause the fluorescence quenching of BSA-AuNCs. Subsequently, the introduction of PM inhibited the activity of AChE and restrained the formation of thiocholine, and the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs was gradually restored. Under the optimal test conditions, the fabricated nanosensors exhibited a linear response range from 0.33 ng/mL to 6.67 ng/mL and a LOD of 0.14 ng/mL.

Another similar method was employed by Yang et al. (2020) for carbendazim quantification in apple and strawberry samples. The FRET effect between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and AuNCs triggered the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs. The introduction of carbendazim activated the aggregation of AuNCs, which effectively inhibited the FRET process and thus the fluorescence of N-CQDs was restored. The fabricated sensors had a linear response range of 1–100 μM and an ultralow LOD of 0.83 μM.

Some researchers use 3D printed smartphones as mobile fluorescence readers for quantitative analysis of pesticides. For instance, Chu et al. (2020) established a portable smartphone platform for thiram detection in milk and apples by an “on-off-on” sensor (Fig. 4C). First, the red CdTe QDs (rQDs) were coated in SiO2 nanoparticles as internal references, and the blue CDs (bCDs) were covalently embedded on the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles as signal report units. The fluorescence of bCDs was quenched by AuNPs due to the FRET effect. Upon the addition of thiram, the fluorescence of bCDs was recovered owing to the formation of the Au–S bond between AuNPs and thiram. Fig. 4D reveals the application of the platform for thiram detection. The smartphone sensing platform was designed by the CAD program and printed through a 3D printer, and the paper strip could accomplish visual detection. The colour recognizer application (APP) installed in the smartphone could read out the red (R), green (G), and blue (B) channel values of the generated images and the R/B values were used for thiram quantification with a LOD of 59 nM.

Bacterial infectious diseases caused by foodborne pathogenic bacteria are highly important health problems all over the world, which pose a significant threat to human health (Sharifi et al., 2020; Jayan et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021), and similarly, “on-off-on” sensors have been developed to determine pathogenic bacteria.

Yan et al. (2018) reported a fluorescent sensing platform by synthesizing AuNCs for Escherichia coli (E. coli) detection in drinking water samples (Fig. 4E). The fluorescent sensor was established by the bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized fluorescent AuNCs to stabilize PL intensity. Initially, Cu2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of AuNCs without interference from other common metal ions, which resulted in the excited state of AuNCs losing its energy. To adapt to copper-rich environments, E. coli could efficiently bind toxic Cu2+ and quickly reduce it to Cu+ through copper-binding system and redox enzyme cascades. Consequently, in the presence of E. coli, Cu2+ could be quickly removed from AuNCs surface and reduced to Cu+ by E. coli via copper-binding and redox pathways, resulting in the fluorescence restoration of AuNCs. The sensing platform showed a LOD of 100 CFU/mL. Besides, Hunsur Ravikumar et al. (2019) employed antibody-conjugated QDs as fluorescent probes and graphene oxides (GOs) as quenchers to detect Staphylococcus aureus in bread and milk samples and obtained an ultralow LOD of 0.3 ng/mL.

As toxic metabolites of mould, mycotoxins extensively exist in agricultural products such as cereals, wheat, corn and peanuts due to inappropriate storage, posing great threats to human health (Wu et al., 2021; He et al., 2020). At present, numerous researches were proposed for the detection of mycotoxins based on “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors.

Wang, Chen, et al. (2016) designed an “on-off-on” sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and aptamer modified gold nanoparticles (Aptamer-AuNPs) for the monitoring of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in peanut and corn samples. To start with, the prepared N-CDs were embedded on aptamer-AuNPs, which led to the remarkable fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. With the introduction of AFB1, aptamer recognized and captured AFB1, which brought out the release of N-CDs and accompanied dynamical fluorescence restoration. Accordingly, the concentration of AFB1 could be calculated based on the degree of fluorescence restoration. The assaying method exhibited a linear range from 5 pg/mL to 2.00 ng/mL and a LOD of 5 pg/mL (16 pM).

Similarly, Liu et al. (2020) proposed an “on-off-on” sensor for rapid monitoring of ochratoxin A (OTA) based on ZnCdSe QDs and self-assembled zinc porphyrin (Fig. 4F). Initially, zinc 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra(4-pyridyl)-21H-23H-porphine (ZnTPyP) could be self-assembled into nanorods (SA-ZnTPyP), which could dynamically quench the fluorescence of ZnCdSe QDs through the PET effect. When OTA was present, the nitrogen/zinc coordination between SA-ZnTPyP and OTA resulted in the release of ZnCdSe QDs from the SA-ZnTPyP surface, and thus restored the fluorescence of ZnCdSe QDs. The assaying method exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards OTA with a linear response range of 0.5–80 ng/mL as well as a LOD of 0.33 ng/mL and the feasibility was verified in milk and coffee samples.

Heavy metal ions have extremely serious biotoxicity after a long time in taking and accumulating by food chains in human bodies. As one of the most hazardous and omnipresent pollutants, Hg2+ can cause severely toxicological effects on human bodies mainly through the internal organs, digestive system and central nervous system (Zhang et al., 2019). Many studies have therefore been performed for the detection of Hg2+ based on “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors.

Cui et al. (2015) considered the conformational change of DNA aptamer induced by Hg2+ and constructed an assaying platform based on CDs-labeled oligodeoxyribonucleotide (ODN-CDs) (Fig. 4E). The fluorescence of resultant ODN-CDs was specifically quenched by GOs via the FRET effect. With the introduction of Hg2+, the aptamer captured Hg2+ and formed thymine-Hg2+- thymine duplex, which led to the release of ODN-CDs from GOs surface and the fluorescence was recovered gradually. Under the optimal conditions, there was a linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 5–200 nM with a LOD of 2.6 nM. The practicability of the assaying method was verified in the sample of citrus leaves and reasonable spiking recoveries were obtained in the range of 94.68%–109.8%.

Similarly, Qian et al. (2015) took advantage of Pb2+-induced G-quadruplex formation and proposed an assaying method for the determination of Pb2+ (Fig. 4F). GOs initially quenched the fluorescence of aptamer-modified GQDs via the PET effect. In the presence of Pb2+ ions, the free Pb2+ ions were captured by aptamer-modified GQDs and formed Pb2+-induced G-quadruplex, which triggered the release of GQDs and thus the fluorescence was restored. The method exhibited a linear response ranging from 9.9 to 435.0 nM with a LOD of 0.6 nM.

In this review, various detecting strategies including redox reaction, competitive coordination reaction, DNA hybridization reaction and enzymatic reaction are presented. Recent advancements of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors in food quality and safety detection are summarized. These studies showed that the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors exhibited excellent linear responses and satisfactory LODs to analytes and the practicability of the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors was mostly and effectively verified in real food samples. Therefore it is anticipated these methods should have great potentials for industrial applications in food analysis in future.

Although great endeavours have been exerted to design “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors, limitations and challenges still remain. Thus, more studies should be performed to overcome these issues for their broad applications in food analysis (Fig. 5 ).
(1)
In some studies, the fluorescence signals of nanomaterials are unstable, which decreases the accuracy of detection. Consequently, more efforts ought to be devoted to the synthesis of nanomaterials with excellent stability. For achieving this, organics such as GSH and BSA and ligands can be modified on the surface of nanomaterials to improve photostability. In addition, the combination of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors with other techniques including immunoassay, electrochemical sensors, colourimetry and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) should improve the accuracy and broaden the implementation range. Therefore muti-modes sensing-based fluorescence can be a promising research direction for improving the analytical performance of fluorescent sensors.
(2)
Although “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors possess the merits of low LODs, large detection range, and strong anti-interference ability, the detection time is longer and the procedure is more complicated than “on-off” fluorescent sensors. Thus, more efforts should be made to shorten detection time and simplify the detection procedures. For example, the application of multi-emission fluorescence can facilely realize visual detection and provide on-site and real-time detection results for rapid food safety detection.
(3)
Currently, the success in the detection based on “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors is mainly limited to laboratory conditions, real-time and on-site detection for commercial and industrial applications is far more difficult and complicated. Therefore, more endeavours should be performed to develop techniques and methods for achieving real-time and on-site detection with “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors. In addition, portable sensors based on fluorescent techniques have recently emerged and attracted widespread attention, thus future research can focus on developing portable applications of “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors including kits and filter papers based sensors, and apps on smartphones can also be developed for rapid and convenient detection of harmful substances and food components.
(4)
Most of the studies focus on food safety analytes, the analysis of nutritional components and other food quality attributes are comparatively rare, especially the analysis of phytochemicals. With the further developments in technology, “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors should be increasingly developed for food quality monitoring in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (3217161084) for its support. This research was also supported by the Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Intelligent Sensing and Quality Control of Agricultural Products (202102100009)), the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Plan Projects (2020A1414010160), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2020A1515010936), the Contemporary International Collaborative Research Centre of Guangdong Province on Food Innovative Processing and Intelligent Control (2019A050519001) and the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products (2021KJ145).
